metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Enumerate all store-touching pure intercepts in `_main_dispatch` that the central gate must cover (at minimum: `verify-identity`, `verify-authorship`, `verify-opcert`, `trusted-env`, `remote-cert`, `enrich`) and add at least one test per intercept (or one parametrized test) asserting auto-mount behavior in a fresh linked worktree, so AC2 ('no store-touching command can skip it') is verifiable.
evidence: Plan says: 'The mount runs at a single central CLI gate covering the pure intercepts; no store-touching command can skip it' (AC2) — but which intercepts beyond verify-commit-ticket are in scope is never stated.
verify-identity: `if not os.path.isdir(tracker): print(...'ticket store not found'...)` at src/rebar/_commands/verify_authorship.py:518-525 — no ensure_initialized call anywhere in its dispatch path.
verify-opcert: same pattern at src/rebar/_commands/verify_opcert.py:161-167.
trusted-env: calls `_tip_position(repo_root)` which reads from the store at src/rebar/_commands/trusted_env_cmd.py:87,116 — no mount step.
remote-cert: calls `persist_envelope` -> `append_event` (a store write) at src/rebar/_commands/remote_cert.py:79,119 — no mount step.
enrich: passes `_enrich_config.tracker_dir()` directly to cmd_enrich at src/rebar/_cli/__init__.py:427-429 — no ensure_initialized call.
AC1 regression test covers only verify-commit-ticket; AC2 says 'no store-touching command can skip it' but that is not verified by a test for the other intercepts.
findings.finding: The plan introduces a central CLI gate to cover 'pure intercepts' and 'every store-touching command', but only names `verify-commit-ticket` as the target. At least five other store-touching pure intercepts in `_main_dispatch` have the same `os.path.isdir(tracker)` without mount bug and are unenumerated: `verify-identity`/`verify-authorship` (verify_authorship.py:518), `verify-opcert` (verify_opcert.py:161), `trusted-env` (reads store tip position via `_tip_position`), `remote-cert` (writes to store via `append_event`), and `enrich` (passes `tracker_dir()` to `cmd_enrich`). An implementer reading only AC1 could write the gate and test only for `verify-commit-ticket`, leaving the other intercepts still broken in fresh linked worktrees.
scenarios: Developer implements the gate and tests only verify-commit-ticket (per AC1), ships, and verify-identity / verify-opcert still fail with 'ticket store not found' in fresh linked worktrees.
CI merge-gate job running `rebar verify-identity` in a freshly checked-out worktree without a pre-mounted store fails on a different error path that the gate fix didn't cover.
findings.suggested_fix: In the Design section, list the full set of store-touching intercepts under the gate's scope. In the Acceptance Criteria, expand AC2's guard test to cover at least `verify-identity` and `verify-opcert` (the other CI merge-gate commands most likely to hit this in practice), or add an explicit AC for 'all store-touching intercepts auto-mount'.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Enumerate all store-touching pure intercepts beyond `verify-commit-ticket` (`verify-identity`, `verify-authorship`, `verify-opcert`, `trusted-env`, `remote-cert`) in the Design section and explicitly state whether each falls inside the central gate or inside the policy=none exemption list, so AC2 ('no store-touching command can skip mount') is fully specified.
evidence: verify-identity cli() calls `config.tracker_dir(args.root)` and exits 2 if the store is missing (verify_authorship.py:517–525) — same pattern as verify-commit-ticket
verify-opcert cli() calls `config.tracker_dir(args.root)` and exits 2 if store missing (verify_opcert.py:160–167)
trusted-env calls `tracker_dir(repo_root)` in `_tip_position` (trusted_env_cmd.py:39)
remote-cert's `persist_envelope` appends a SIGNATURE event via `append_event` — a store write (remote_cert.py:119)
Plan exemption list: 'init (is init), scratch (fs-only), explain (guides, no store), and pure no-store reads' — does not mention any of the above four commands
findings.finding: The plan names `verify-commit-ticket` as the only failing pure intercept but there are at least four additional store-touching pure intercepts in the same `_main_dispatch` region (`verify-identity`/`verify-authorship`, `verify-opcert`, `trusted-env`, `remote-cert`). The plan's exemption list ('init, scratch, explain, and pure no-store reads') does not enumerate which intercepts qualify as 'pure no-store reads', leaving the implementer unable to verify AC2 ('no store-touching command can skip mount') without guessing the scope of the gate.
findings.location: Design section — 'Commands that must NOT auto-mount (policy=none): init, scratch, explain, and pure no-store reads' / AC2 — 'no store-touching command can skip it'
scenarios: Implementer places the central gate before verify-commit-ticket but accidentally places it after the verify-identity/verify-opcert/trusted-env/remote-cert intercepts — AC2 remains false for those commands in a fresh worktree.
Implementer treats 'pure no-store reads' as covering verify-identity (since it only reads), and therefore exempts it — but then verify-identity still fails in a fresh worktree.
findings.suggested_fix: Add a sentence to the Design section such as: 'The following intercepts are ALSO store-touching and fall inside the gate: verify-identity, verify-authorship (alias), verify-opcert, trusted-env (reads store tip), remote-cert (writes SIGNATURE event). None are policy=none. The policy=none set is exactly: init, scratch, explain, grounding-info, bridge-probe, config, llm, prompt, criteria, jira-onboard, and any intercept whose entire logic is repo-independent.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Enumerate the 'pure no-store reads' commands explicitly in the Design section (or state 'none beyond init/scratch/explain') so the executor has a complete policy=none list to implement and test.
evidence: 'pure no-store reads' — no enumeration or definition of which CLI commands belong to this category
The Acceptance Criteria AC3 only names 'explain (and init/scratch)' as guard-tested exemptions, leaving the 'pure no-store reads' bucket untested and undefined
findings.finding: The Design section names 'pure no-store reads' as a policy=none category beyond the three named exemptions (init, scratch, explain), but never enumerates which commands qualify. An executor must decide which additional commands get the exemption without guidance.
findings.location: Design section — 'Commands that must NOT auto-mount (policy=none): init (is init), scratch (fs-only), explain (guides, no store), and pure no-store reads.'
scenarios: Executor adds the gate and must decide whether e.g. `rebar help`, `rebar version`, `rebar status` (if it exists) fall in this category — different choices produce different behavior with no spec to adjudicate.
findings.suggested_fix: Replace 'and pure no-store reads' with an explicit parenthetical, e.g. '(none beyond these three — all other CLI commands touch the store)' or list the additional commands by name if they exist.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a concrete proving command or test for AC2 — e.g. 'a unit test that stubs `_create_tracker` and asserts it is called for every store-touching command, including verify-commit-ticket, before dispatch' — so structural centralization is machine-verifiable.
evidence: AC2 text: 'The mount runs at a single central CLI gate covering the pure intercepts; no store-touching command can skip it.' — no test name, no command, no grep anchor, no structural assertion described
AC5 provides 'make lint && make typecheck && make test green' but a passing test suite does not prove structural centralization — tests prove behavior, not that a gate cannot be bypassed
findings.finding: AC2 ('The mount runs at a single central CLI gate covering the pure intercepts; no store-touching command can skip it') is a structural code property with no stated proving command. The executor cannot confirm this criterion is met without code inspection or a comprehensive integration test, neither of which is specified.
scenarios: The gate is implemented but one store-touching intercept still bypasses it; the test suite passes because the bypass path happens to work via another route; AC2 is silently false.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Strengthen AC1 to require an end-to-end test that creates a real temporary git worktree (no mock of `_create_tracker` / `ensure_initialized`) and asserts the symlink is actually created and the ticket resolves — explicitly excluding mock-based substitutes for the mount boundary.
evidence: 'regression test' is named but no constraint is placed on whether the test uses a real FS worktree or a mock substitute
The bug was discovered in a live linked worktree (real symlink scenario); a mocked test would not have caught it
Cutover: the new default is auto-mount before intercepts; proxy-validation of this defaulted path is a MAJOR E5 failure
findings.finding: The regression test in AC1 ('verify-commit-ticket in a fresh linked worktree auto-mounts') is the critical acceptance path for the newly-defaulted gate, but the plan does not specify that this test must use a real temporary git worktree and real symlink creation — leaving it satisfiable by a mock/stub that short-circuits `_create_tracker`, so the suite can go green while the live mount path is still broken.
scenarios: Test author uses unittest.mock to stub ensure_initialized/init_only=True; test passes; real worktree scenario still fails in CI/production
Future refactor changes _create_tracker internals; mock-based test stays green; regression reintroduced
findings.suggested_fix: Add to AC1: 'Test MUST use a real temporary git repo + linked worktree (e.g. via subprocess or pytest tmp_path + git worktree add); it MUST assert the .tickets-tracker symlink is physically created and the ticket ID resolves — mocking _create_tracker or ensure_initialized is not permitted for this test.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Replace or supplement the structural AC2 assertion with a behavioral integration test: for each known pure-intercept command (at minimum `verify-commit-ticket`), assert that invoking it in a store-absent environment actually produces the side-effect of mounting (symlink created or REBAR_TRACKER_DIR recognized), not merely that a call-site exists in the source.
evidence: 'The mount runs at a single central CLI gate covering the pure intercepts; no store-touching command can skip it' — the observable outcome is structural, inviting a grep-based test
No behavioral/runtime observable is named for this criterion (e.g. 'running command X with no store and REBAR_TRACKER_DIR unset produces symlink Y')
findings.finding: AC2 ('no store-touching command can skip the central gate') could be satisfied by a source-grep or structural assertion that reads the source file and checks for a call-site, which is a change-detector / source-grep-masquerading-as-behavioral-test anti-pattern — it would lock in the structural shape without verifying runtime behavior.
scenarios: Test asserts that `_cli/__init__.py` contains the string `ensure_initialized(init_only=True)` before the intercept block; refactor moves the call to a helper; test still greens; a new intercept added later skips the helper

evidence: 1 criterion line(s) bundle multiple deliverables with 'and' (split so each is independently verifiable).
findings.suggested_fix: Split compound criteria, replace subjective terms with observable outcomes, and state how each is verified.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the concrete test command for AC1 (e.g. `pytest tests/test_cli_init.py::test_verify_commit_ticket_fresh_worktree -v`) and for AC3 (e.g. `pytest tests/test_cli_init.py::test_explain_no_store -v`) so done-ness is machine-verifiable.
evidence: AC1: '...auto-mounts (symlink to main repo store) and resolves the id, instead of "ticket store not found" — regression test.' — no path, no function name, no command
AC3: '...they keep working with no store — guard test.' — similarly unnamed
findings.finding: AC1 and AC3 reference 'regression test' and 'guard test' respectively without naming the test file, test function, or the command to run them in isolation. A reviewer cannot confirm these tests exist and pass, nor can the executor know what constitutes a satisfying test.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — AC1 ('regression test'), AC3 ('guard test')
scenarios: Executor writes a test that exercises a mocked path; the real CLI gate is bypassed in the test harness; the AC is marked done but the fix is unproven in a realistic linked-worktree scenario.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Enumerate ALL store-touching pure intercepts (verify-identity, verify-opcert, trusted-env, remote-cert, enrich) and either confirm they are covered by the central gate or explicitly scope them out with rationale in the Design section — AC2 as written implies full coverage.
evidence: Plan: 'The mount runs at a single central CLI gate covering the pure intercepts; no store-touching command can skip it.'
Plan AC2: 'The mount runs at a single central CLI gate covering the pure intercepts; no store-touching command can skip it.'
verify-identity intercept at __init__.py:448 dispatches to verify_authorship.cli() — verify_authorship.py:518 does os.path.isdir(tracker) without ensure_initialized
verify-opcert intercept at __init__.py:456 dispatches to verify_opcert.cli() — verify_opcert.py:161 does os.path.isdir(tracker) without ensure_initialized
enrich intercept at __init__.py:425-429 calls cmd_enrich(argv[1:], str(_enrich_config.tracker_dir())) with no ensure_initialized
trusted-env and remote-cert intercepts at __init__.py:460-467 route to store-reading commands without ensure_initialized
Only AC1 regression-tests verify-commit-ticket; AC2's 'no command can skip it' claim is not grounded in an enumeration or per-intercept test
findings.finding: The plan asserts 'no store-touching command can skip [the central gate]' (AC2) and that the gate covers 'the pure intercepts' (plural), but code inspection shows at least five additional pure intercepts — verify-identity (__init__.py:448), verify-authorship (__init__.py:452), verify-opcert (__init__.py:456), trusted-env (__init__.py:460), remote-cert (__init__.py:464), and enrich (__init__.py:425) — each resolve and check the tracker store via os.path.isdir(tracker) without any ensure_initialized call, the same structural pattern as the verify-commit-ticket bug. The plan names only verify-commit-ticket in AC1's regression test and does not enumerate which intercepts the central gate covers or confirm these others are deliberately excluded.
findings.location: Design section ('covering the pure intercepts') and AC2 ('no store-touching command can skip it'); cross-referenced src/rebar/_cli/__init__.py:425-464
scenarios: rebar verify-identity in a fresh linked worktree fails with 'ticket store not found' for the same structural reason as verify-commit-ticket — after this plan lands, only verify-commit-ticket is fixed
rebar enrich in a fresh worktree hits the same missing-mount failure path
The broad AC2 claim passes review but the implementation leaves a class of store-touching intercepts unaddressed
findings.suggested_fix: Add a table in the Design section listing every `if argv and argv[0] == "...":` intercept, its store-touch status (yes/no), and its gate policy (covered / policy=none / explicitly deferred). If verify-identity and verify-opcert are intentionally excluded (e.g. because CI always mounts the store before running them), say so — otherwise extend the central gate to cover them and add a regression test per AC1's model.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an acceptance criterion (or extend AC #3) that explicitly guards 'pure no-store reads' commands against auto-mounting, matching the policy=none list stated in the Design section.
evidence: Design: 'Commands that must NOT auto-mount (policy=none): init (is init), scratch (fs-only), explain (guides, no store), and pure no-store reads'
AC #3: 'explain (and init/scratch) are NOT force-mounted — they keep working with no store — guard test' — omits 'pure no-store reads' entirely
findings.finding: The Design section lists 'pure no-store reads' as a fourth policy=none exemption alongside init/scratch/explain, but no acceptance criterion covers this category — there is no guard test verifying that pure no-store read commands still work without a store after the gate is introduced.
scenarios: A pure no-store read command is accidentally enrolled in the gate; the guard test in AC #3 does not catch the regression because it only checks explain/init/scratch.
findings.suggested_fix: Extend AC #3 to read: 'explain, init, scratch, and pure no-store read commands are NOT force-mounted — they keep working with no store — guard test for each category.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify that `_create_tracker` (or the gate wrapper) handles concurrent symlink creation safely — either by catching `FileExistsError`/`errno.EEXIST` and treating it as success, or by documenting that the existing implementation already does so.
evidence: Plan states re-mount is 'idempotent' but idempotency under sequential retry does not imply safety under concurrent execution
Symlink creation via os.symlink is not atomic with a prior existence check — classic TOCTOU
No mention of locking, CAS, or catching FileExistsError at the gate
findings.finding: Concurrent CLI invocations in the same worktree can race on the symlink/branch-attach creation (TOCTOU): two processes may both observe 'no symlink' and attempt `os.symlink` simultaneously; one will raise `FileExistsError`. The plan acknowledges idempotency for sequential re-mount but does not specify atomic handling for this concurrent case.
findings.location: Design section: 'The MOUNT half of init (`ensure_initialized(init_only=True)` → `_create_tracker`: symlink a worktree to the main repo store'
scenarios: Two parallel `rebar` commands (e.g., pre-commit hooks running in parallel) both hit the gate in an uninitialized worktree; one fails with FileExistsError that propagates as an unhandled exception
findings.suggested_fix: In the gate call site (or in `_create_tracker`), catch `FileExistsError` on symlink creation and treat it as a successful no-op, making concurrent invocations safe without locks.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an explicit rollback/back-out plan (e.g., a feature flag, revert procedure, or expand-contract sequencing) for the central gate insertion so that the gate can be disabled without a full revert if it causes unexpected regressions.
evidence: Design: 'MOUNT half of init should run at ONE central CLI choke point, BEFORE both the pure intercepts and the set-based dispatch, for every store-touching command'
No section describes how to revert the gate if it causes unexpected failures in production worktrees or embedded contexts.
REBAR_TRACKER_DIR is mentioned as a short-circuit for embedding tests, but not framed as a rollback escape hatch for operators.
findings.finding: The plan inserts a mandatory mount gate before all CLI dispatch (a behavioral change for every store-touching command), but provides no explicit rollback plan or expand-contract sequencing if the gate itself causes regressions — T4 requires a reversibility mechanism, not just acknowledgement of scope.
scenarios: The central gate has a latent bug (e.g., it mounts in an embedded context that sets no REBAR_TRACKER_DIR override, or it mounts during a command that should be policy=none but wasn't listed); with no rollback path, the only remedy is a hotfix release.
findings.suggested_fix: Add a short 'Rollback' section: e.g., 'The gate can be disabled by reverting the single insertion point in _cli/__init__.py; REBAR_TRACKER_DIR=<any path> can serve as an operator escape hatch in emergencies.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a note to AGENTS.md (or a lightweight ADR) documenting the central CLI gate pattern and the 'policy=none' exemption contract so future command authors know when to opt out.
evidence: 'Commands that must NOT auto-mount (policy=none): init (is init), scratch (fs-only), explain (guides, no store), and pure no-store reads' — hardcoded in plan prose only
AC item 'docs/cli-reference regenerated if the CLI surface changed' does not cover internal architectural decisions
No ADR, AGENTS.md, or design-doc update mentioned anywhere in the plan
findings.finding: The central CLI gate and its 'policy=none' exemption list (init, scratch, explain, pure no-store reads) constitute a new architectural contract that every future command author must know about, but no ADR, AGENTS.md update, or design-doc note is planned to capture it.
findings.location: Design section / Acceptance Criteria — no doc/ADR update listed
scenarios: A developer adds a new guide/diagnostic command that touches no store but is dispatched through the same path, doesn't know about the policy=none contract, and inadvertently triggers an auto-mount causing errors or side-effects in restricted environments.
A reviewer months later cannot find why certain commands skip the gate, because the rationale lives only in git history.
findings.suggested_fix: Add a short section to AGENTS.md: 'CLI Mount Gate: ensure_initialized(init_only=True) runs at the CLI choke-point before all dispatch. Commands that must NOT auto-mount (currently: init, scratch, explain, pure no-store reads) must be listed in the exemption set [location]. When adding a new command, decide and document its mount policy here.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Define the exemption set as a named constant or use a decorator/metadata attribute on handler functions so new commands can self-declare their mount policy rather than requiring edits to the central gate logic.
evidence: 'Commands that must NOT auto-mount (policy=none): init (is init), scratch (fs-only), explain (guides, no store), and pure no-store reads' — enumerated in prose, no extensibility mechanism described
No mention of a decorator, metadata flag, registry, or named constant for the exemption set
findings.finding: The 'policy=none' exemption list is described as a fixed set of named commands (init, scratch, explain, plus 'pure no-store reads') with no described mechanism for future commands to declare their exemption — meaning the list must be edited in an opaque location whenever a new exempt command is added.
findings.location: Design section — 'Commands that must NOT auto-mount (policy=none)'
scenarios: New 'rebar help-topic' or similar guide command is added; developer must discover and edit the exemption set in the gate implementation, with no compile-time or test-time guard that they forgot to do so.
findings.suggested_fix: E.g., a module-level set NO_MOUNT_COMMANDS = {'init', 'scratch', 'explain'} referenced by the gate, or a @no_store_mount decorator on the handler, so the policy is co-located with the command definition and grep-able.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify error-handling behavior at the central gate: define what error is raised (and its message) when `ensure_initialized(init_only=True)` fails, confirm it is surfaced to the user (not swallowed), and add at least a warning-level log at the gate failure point.
evidence: Plan says 'idempotent re-mount is cheap and non-fatal' for per-arm calls but makes no claim about gate-level failure behavior
No error-surfacing or logging specification for when _create_tracker fails at the gate
AC covers the happy path (auto-mounts and resolves id) but not the gate-failure path
findings.finding: The plan introduces a central gate that performs symlink/branch-attach file I/O on every store-touching command invocation, but specifies no error-handling behavior for gate-level mount failures (e.g., main repo store missing, permission denied on symlink, corrupted tickets branch). It is unspecified whether failures are surfaced as a clear error to the user or swallowed, and no observability (log/metric) is prescribed.
findings.location: Design section / AC line 2: 'The mount runs at a single central CLI gate'
scenarios: User runs `rebar verify-commit-ticket` in a worktree where the main repo store is genuinely absent or inaccessible — gate fails silently or with a cryptic traceback instead of a clear diagnostic
CI environment with restricted FS permissions causes gate symlink to fail; operator has no log/metric to diagnose why all store-touching commands began failing
findings.suggested_fix: Wrap the gate call in a try/except that catches mount errors and re-raises as a `RebarUserError` (or equivalent) with a message like 'Could not mount ticket store: <reason>. Run `rebar init` to initialize.' Log at WARNING level before re-raising.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an AC or design note specifying the user-facing error message when the central gate cannot mount the store, ensuring it is more actionable than the previous 'ticket store not found' and guides the user toward resolution (e.g., 'Run `rebar init` in the main repo first').
evidence: AC line 1 covers only the success case: 'auto-mounts (symlink to main repo store) and resolves the id'
No AC or design note specifies the error message/UX when gate mount fails (the abandon/error exit)
The prior error was 'ticket store not found' — after this change, failure mode may change in ways not specified
findings.finding: The plan specifies the UX for the happy path (auto-mount succeeds, command proceeds) and the guard cases (explain/init/scratch skip the gate), but does not specify the user-facing error message or flow when auto-mount genuinely cannot complete (e.g., no main repo store, orphaned worktree, no tickets branch). The user's prior experience was 'ticket store not found'; the plan doesn't define what replaces that for a gate-level failure.
findings.location: AC line 1 and Design section: policy=none list
scenarios: Developer in an orphaned worktree (main repo store deleted) runs any store-touching command; the new gate fails with an unspecified error that may be worse UX than the previous 'ticket store not found'

metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)
